title: Listening - Part 3: A Museum Exhibition
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about an exhibition they recently visited. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what impressed each speaker most about the exhibition. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about an exhibition they recently visited. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what impressed each speaker most about the exhibition. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about visiting an exhibition.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I went to an exhibition about inventions with my college class. I expected rows of machines in glass cases and long written explanations. There were some of those, but they weren’t what I remembered afterwards. [19]Visitors could build a simple circuit, programme a small vehicle and test different materials in a wind tunnel, so we actually experienced the ideas rather than only reading about them.[/19] My friends and I spent two hours in that section, and even our teacher had trouble persuading us to leave.||Speaker 2 Man: The town museum held an exhibition about life a hundred years ago. Nothing looked especially valuable: there were shopping lists, school exercise books, a worker’s lunch box and an old wedding dress. [20]What fascinated me was reading who had owned each object and how it had been used during important moments in those people's lives.[/20] Suddenly the past felt much more real than when I learn dates in a history lesson. I even returned with my grandmother a week later.||Speaker 3 Woman: I visited a modern art exhibition because my cousin wanted to go. On my own, I often look at abstract paintings and feel that I’m missing the point. We joined a short tour. [21]The guide didn’t tell us what to think, but explained the techniques the artists had used and asked questions that helped me notice details I would otherwise have walked straight past.[/21] I still didn’t like every picture, but for the first time I enjoyed discussing this kind of art.||Speaker 4 Man: The photography exhibition showed images of storms and extreme weather. The pictures were powerful, but I’ve seen dramatic photographs online before. [22]What made this exhibition different was entering dark rooms where recordings of wind and rain played around you while light changed across the photographs, making you feel almost inside the scene.[/22] My younger brother found it slightly frightening, but I thought it was clever. It turned a collection of still images into a complete experience.||Speaker 5 Woman: I took my eight-year-old niece to an exhibition about ancient Egypt and worried she'd be bored after ten minutes. There were plenty of historical objects for adults to examine. [23]But the organisers had also created a trail of mysteries for children to solve, costumes to try on and a space where they could design their own symbols.[/23] My niece was engaged for the entire afternoon and kept explaining facts to her parents afterwards. It was wonderful to see a museum designed with children genuinely in mind.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I went to an exhibition about inventions with my college class. I expected rows of machines in glass cases and long written explanations. There were some of those, but they weren’t what I remembered afterwards. Visitors could build a simple circuit, programme a small vehicle and test different materials in a wind tunnel, so we actually experienced the ideas rather than only reading about them. My friends and I spent two hours in that section, and even our teacher had trouble persuading us to leave.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: The town museum held an exhibition about life a hundred years ago. Nothing looked especially valuable: there were shopping lists, school exercise books, a worker’s lunch box and an old wedding dress. What fascinated me was reading who had owned each object and how it had been used during important moments in those people's lives. Suddenly the past felt much more real than when I learn dates in a history lesson. I even returned with my grandmother a week later.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I visited a modern art exhibition because my cousin wanted to go. On my own, I often look at abstract paintings and feel that I’m missing the point. We joined a short tour. The guide didn’t tell us what to think, but explained the techniques the artists had used and asked questions that helped me notice details I would otherwise have walked straight past. I still didn’t like every picture, but for the first time I enjoyed discussing this kind of art.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: The photography exhibition showed images of storms and extreme weather. The pictures were powerful, but I’ve seen dramatic photographs online before. What made this exhibition different was entering dark rooms where recordings of wind and rain played around you while light changed across the photographs, making you feel almost inside the scene. My younger brother found it slightly frightening, but I thought it was clever. It turned a collection of still images into a complete experience.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I took my eight-year-old niece to an exhibition about ancient Egypt and worried she'd be bored after ten minutes. There were plenty of historical objects for adults to examine. But the organisers had also created a trail of mysteries for children to solve, costumes to try on and a space where they could design their own symbols. My niece was engaged for the entire afternoon and kept explaining facts to her parents afterwards. It was wonderful to see a museum designed with children genuinely in mind.

options.text: the chance to try things themselves
the way it entertained young visitors
the use of sound and lighting
the opportunity to buy handmade goods

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker was most impressed by the interactive practical activities (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker appreciated learning the personal histories connected with everyday items (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker found that a guide made modern artworks easier and more interesting to understand (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker particularly noticed how lighting and recorded sounds enhanced the photographs (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker was pleased that the exhibition provided enjoyable and educational activities for children (evidence [23]).
